INT. SIMPSON HOUSE - LIVING ROOM - LATER Scene as Homer is sulking. HOMER I had a feeling it was too good to be true. Every time you get a million dollars something queers the deal. LISA (EYEING CHECK) I don’t think real checks have exclamation points. MARGE (HOLDING MAGAZINE) Well, at least we got a free sample of Reading Digest. Marge hands it to him. HOMER Marge, I’ve never read a magazine in my life and I’m not gonna start now. Something catches his eye. HOMER (CONT’D)

Hey, a cartoon.

8F01 REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/08/91

ON CARTOON - "MOTORING MS.-HAPS" (Cont >)

A WOMAN is standing on the street next to her car which is crumpled up like an accordion. She’s trying to calm her angry HUSBAND. HOMER (READING CAPTION) "Well dear, you always said you wanted a compact." -- heh-heh-heh. Ain’t it the truth. MARGE (FIRMLY) No, it is not the truth, Homer. It’s well documented that women are safer drivers than men. HOMER Oh, Marge. Cartoons don’t have any deep meaning. They’re just stupid drawings that give you a cheap laugh.

Homer stands up and we see the crack of his butt over his pants.

INT. BURN’S OFFICE ~- CONTINUOUS

Homer is on a surveillance monitor. PULL BACK to reveal BURNS and SMITHERS watching him.

BURNS Who is that bookworm, Smithers? SMITHERS Uh, Homer Simpson, sir. BURNS Simpson, eh? How very strange. His job description clearly specifies an illiterate. INT. SIMPSON HOUSE - LIVING ROOM - EVENING

Bart and Lisa are watching TV. Homer is reading his magazine.

TV ANNOUNCER (V.0.) We now return to Troy McClure and Delores Montenegro in "Preacher with

a Shovel."

8F01 REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/08/91 6.

Sce ne 3 Ccont )

HOMER Marge, look at them staring at the idiot box. God forbid they would ever read something and improve their minds.

MARGE Homer, you’ve certainly taken a shine to that magazine.

HOMER It’s not just one magazine, Marge. It’s hundreds of magazines. Everything from "Better Lawns and Hedges" to "Modern Backache". It gives me the warm feeling I used to get when my mom cut up my meat for me. (POINTING TO THE KIDS) They don’t know what they’re missing.

Homer turns off the TV.

LISA Daaad!

BART What gives, man?

HOMER We're all gonna sit down as a family and listen to an inspiring story of

wilderness survival.

Homer is absorbed in another article: "They Call Me ‘Doctor Soybean.’" He’s wearing reading glasses.

I thought you might want to snuggle.

Getting an idea, Lisa starts to write. Scone? EXT. VETERANS OF POPULAR WARS HALL - THAT SATURDAY Lisa and Homer walk through the parking lot. LISA Thank you for driving me to the contest, dad. HOMER Sweetheart, there’s nothing I wouldn’t do for that magazine. INT. HALL - MEETING ROOM Homer and Lisa enter the hall. SEVERAL CONTESTANTS are quietly rehearsing their essays as they wait to be called. One BOY is dressed as Paul Revere. Uniformed VETERANS, including JASPER, are the local judges. NELSON is reading. A banner behind him reads: "Essay Contest - Regional Finals". NELSON (READS) "So burn the flag if you must. But before you do, you’d better burn a few other things. You'd better burn your shirt and your pants. Be sure to burn your TV and car. Oh yes, and don’t forget to burn your house. Because none of those things could exist without six white stripes, seven red stripes, and

a hell of a lotta stars." Thank you.

Nelson exits to APPLAUSE. The JUDGES mark their scorecards. Homer CHEERS and WHISTLES.

LISA (READING) In 1776, many of the trees in Springfield Forest were tiny saplings, trembling towards the sun. Lisa walks over to another area. The Paul Revere Boy’s dad is dressing him down.

DAD “We the purple"? What the hell was that? Lisa looks over at Homer as the judge quizzes him. He shrugs vacantly after each question. The judge gives Homer a simple neurological test, asking him to touch his nose with his eyes closed. Homer misses by several feet. After the test, the judge and Homer walk over to Lisa. FEMALE JUDGE Lisa, after meeting your father, I’ve decided to award you an additional five points. Congratulations. You

and your family are going to

Washington!

At a souvenir stand in front of the Pentagon, Homer is posing with a life-sized cardboard cutout of George Bush.

WIDEN to show CONGRESSMAN BOB ARNOLD looking it over. LOBBYIST (0.S.) Congressman, this is Springfield National Forest. WIDEN to show a LOBBYIST from the logging industry is with the congressman. He’s a big, strapping guy wearing a knit cap, lumberjack boots, and a plaid shirt under his traditional three-piece suit. LOBBYIST Basically, what we want to do is cut it down. On the easel, he flips over an artist’s conception of the forest. It depicts a gloomy thicket of gnarled, rotting trees, choking with underbrush and thorny weeds. snakes, buzzards and spiders fill the branches. A cute deer is pinned under a fallen tree. LOBBYIST (CONT’D) As you can see in our artist’s rendition it’s full of old growth, just aging and festering away. In comes our logging company... The next drawing depicts a delightful meadow full of tree stumps. Wildflowers abound; adorable chipmunks, fawns, and

bear cubs cavort in harmony. Several animals are having a tea party on a stump.

LOBBYIST So, Bob, where do we stand on Springfield Forest? Do I get my logging permit?

Lisa pricks up her ears. The lobbyist opens a briefcase to reveal a large amount of cash.

ARNOLD (LOOKING IN BRIEFCASE) Tim-berrr! The lobbyist hands Arnold the briefcase full of cash. They LAUGH together and it ECHOES through the hall. Lisa is horrified. Arnold glances at the statue of Winifred Beecher Howe. ARNOLD (CONT’D) Ugh, what a pooch.

The two men exit as they came. Lisa is shattered. Tearfully, she tears up her essay. The pieces flutter down.

ON ONE PIECE

We can read the title of her essay: "The Roots Of Democracy."

FADE OUT. END_OF ACT TWO

A SMALL CROWD is on hand for the essay finals. They’re seated at round tables decorated with little flags. All CONTESTANTS are seated on the stage except Lisa, whose chair is empty. An irrepressible SONGSTER much like Mark Russell is the entertainment. He’s finishing up a bouncy satirical tune.

Lisa BURSTS through two large doors and strides to the podium.

Uh... okay. .

8F01 REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/08/91 Scene LISA (READING) "The city of Washington was built on a stagnant swamp some 200 years ago, and very little has changed. It stank then, and it stinks now. Lisa is winding up her essay. The audience is now in an UPROAR.

LISA (READING ABOVE NOISE) "And who did I see taking a bribe but my own Congressman, The (AIR QUOTES) "Honorable" Bob Arnold.
